primary_diagnosis.name: Post-EBV ME/CFS pharyngeal flare (reactive lymphadenopathy) — quinsy/airway to be excluded

pathophysiology_summary: Post-EBV immune dysregulation drives recurrent sore throat and reactive cervical lymphadenopathy during ME/CFS flares, often triggered by stress/exertion, mimicking the original glandular fever. The mimics to exclude are peritonsillar abscess (quinsy) and airway compromise, plus bacterial pharyngitis and acute HIV. Reassuring features are a patent airway (able to swallow saliva, no drooling/stridor), normal voice, no trismus, bilateral rather than unilateral involvement, and no high fever/exudate, with a stereotyped prior pattern. However, telehealth cannot examine the oropharynx or palpate nodes, so quinsy cannot be excluded and painful swallowing risks dehydration; the correct action is urgent in-person throat assessment and hydration support (not home NSAID/numbing spray), with 000 for drooling, stridor, muffled voice, trismus or inability to swallow saliva.

differential.evidence_basis: Severe sore throat with odynophagia demands airway/quinsy screening.
Recurrent post-EBV flare is most likely.
Screen fever/exudate; less likely given chronic recurrent pattern.
Sore throat/lymphadenopathy can be seroconversion; consider serology in work-up.
Reassuring airway screen, bilateral involvement, no high fever, stereotyped pattern.
Lower probability but not excludable without an oropharyngeal exam.
Swab if the exam suggests bacterial cause.
Serology as part of the work-up.

differential.diagnosis: Post-EBV ME/CFS pharyngeal flare — quinsy/airway to be excluded
differential.evidence_basis: Working telehealth position: likely a post-EBV flare, but urgent in-person throat assessment and hydration are needed, with airway safety-netting.

red_flags.description: Drooling, inability to swallow saliva, or stridor/noisy breathing
red_flags.clinical_significance: Indicate impending airway obstruction — an emergency.

red_flags.description: Muffled 'hot potato' voice, trismus, or unilateral tonsillar swelling (quinsy)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Signal peritonsillar abscess needing urgent drainage.

red_flags.description: Inability to maintain fluids due to odynophagia (dehydration)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Poor intake risks dehydration and may need assisted hydration.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Attributing severe odynophagia to a flare without excluding quinsy or arranging a throat exam.
Not addressing poor fluid intake from painful swallowing.
Prescribing NSAIDs/numbing spray remotely instead of arranging assessment.
Prescribing antibiotics blindly for a likely viral flare, or withholding assessment where quinsy is possible.

risk_scores.expected_score_or_tier: Reassuring airway screen (swallows saliva, no drooling/stridor/muffled voice/trismus) — quinsy not excluded without exam
risk_scores.clinical_implication: Supports urgent in-person throat assessment with airway safety-netting.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Oropharyngeal examination for exudate/unilateral swelling/uvular deviation
Throat swab/serology and objective hydration status
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Screen airway red flags; arrange urgent in-person throat assessment and hydration support; escalate to 000 for drooling, stridor, muffled voice, trismus or inability to swallow saliva.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Attributing severe odynophagia to a flare and treating remotely could miss quinsy/airway compromise and worsen dehydration.

guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Suspected peritonsillar abscess or airway compromise requires urgent ENT assessment; sore throat management depends on examination and severity.
Recognise post-viral flares and manage with rest/pacing, without dismissing new red-flag features.
